title: HTTP/3, QUIC, QPACK, Migration, and Production Protocol Defense · Concurrent Code Workshop
summary: A protocol pairing room predicts byte, parser, state, ownership, and cleanup transitions before assembling the first multi-version protocol release and incident defense for http/3, quic, qpack, migration, and production protocol defense. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of multi-version protocol release and incident defense while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence d…
objectives: Distinguish QUIC connection IDs, encrypted packets, transport parameters, bidirectional and unidirectional streams, migration, loss recovery, and UDP path behavior.
Trace HTTP/3 control streams, SETTINGS, request streams, frame placement, cancellation, GOAWAY, and connection versus stream errors.
Explain QPACK encoder and decoder streams, dynamic-table capacity, blocked streams, acknowledgements, confidentiality, and decompression limits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Quic Stream Connection Model
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop protocol team handles case 6693 in a canary edge service advertising HTTP/3 with bounded fallback. Build deterministic Go evidence to stage HTTP/3 through bounded QPACK, fallback health, and explicit rollback evidence; preserve this operating constraint: all attacker-controlled counts must be validated before allocation or slicing; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Distinguish QUIC connection IDs, encrypted packets, transport parameters, bidirectional and unidirectional streams, migration, loss recovery, and UDP path behavior.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Quic Stream Connection Model.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish quic connection ids, encrypted packets, transport parameters, bidirectional and unidirectional streams, migration, loss recovery, and udp path behavior. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop Quic Stream Connection Model, keep Quic Stream Connection Model connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Production Protocol Defense
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: httpproto-production-protocol-defense-trdhig" that uses case 2960 to stage HTTP/3 through bounded QPACK, fallback health, and explicit rollback evidence. Browser code must not open sockets or listeners, negotiate TLS, contact networks or proxies, capture packets, read host state, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the named Go 1.26 protocol transfer gates.

Build deterministic Go evidence to stage HTTP/3 through bounded QPACK, fallback health, and explicit rollback evidence; preserve this operating constraint: one connection has exactly one read owner and one terminal close owner; then change certificate identity while ALPN stays valid and reject the connection. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Defend standards scope, byte parsing, framing, transitions, TLS identity, intermediaries, limits, state invariants, tests, observability, version negotiation, deployment, rollback, and residual risk.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Production Protocol Defense symptom.

content.value: HTTP/3 — Controls HTTP over QUIC, control and request streams, settings, frame placement, cancellation, GOAWAY, priorities, and security.

options.text: Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.

For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Production Protocol Defense happy path, omit ownership, error, changed input, goroutine stop, and full-toolchain checks, then accept plausible output.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Production Protocol Defense condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Production Protocol Defense

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Defend standards scope, byte parsing, framing, transitions, TLS identity, intermediaries, limits, state invariants, tests, observability, version negotiation, deployment, rollback, and residual risk.
